zizlfz Notes

    Last updated at March 26, 2026

    How I Build Software

    I believe that "tactical programming", where a software engineer jumps straight into code without fully understanding the problemis, is the root cause of most software failures.

    My approach was fundamentally shaped by the principles in SolidBook, which taught me to elucidate essential complexity and eliminate accidental complexity.

    I follow a rigorous four-phase loop to ensure I am solving the right problem: Discover → Plan → Understand → Build.

    Context of Application

    I apply this mindset in two distinct ways depending on my role:

    • As a Solo Engineer: When handling the full stack, I execute this entire lifecycle—from domain discovery to deployment. This ensures that the product isn't just coded, but that the architecture accurately models the business problem.
    • As a Frontend Engineer: In a team setting, I focus deeply on the Understand and Build phases. I strive to build a mental model of the project by learning the domain and features, even if the backend logic isn't my responsibility. This allows me to implement frontend code that doesn't just "match the design," but reliably handles state, failure cases, and business rules in sync with the system as a whole.

    1. Discover: Learn the Domain

    Before writing a single line of code, I learn the language of the business.

    If developers invent their own terminology, they build the wrong thing. I use Domain-Driven Design (DDD) techniques to build a shared mental model.

    • Event Storming: I map out the business chronologically using sticky notes (Events, Commands, Aggregates) to identify the core subdomains.
    • Ubiquitous Language: I build a glossary to ensure that the codebase speaks the same language as the domain experts (e.g., using "Offer" instead of generic "Transaction").
    • Deliverable: A domain glossary and context map.

    2. Plan: Slice the Work

    A large feature is just a container for smaller problems.

    I break work down into vertical slices of value, not horizontal technical layers.

    • INVEST Stories: Every story must be Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, and Testable.
    • Release Planning: I prioritize based on business value and velocity, ensuring we build the riskiest or most valuable items first.

    3. Understand: The "Secret Sauce"

    This is the most critical phase. I never start coding until the story is fully understood.

    I create three specific design artifacts before implementation. These are written in pseudocode so they are checkable by domain experts—if a non-technical stakeholder can't read it, it's too complex.

    1. Feature Summary I define the contract of the feature, explicitly listing every success and failure state.

    Feature: "Make Offer"
  Namespace: Trading
  Input: Unvalidated Offer
  Output (Success): OfferPlaced event
  Output (Failure): InsufficientCredits, NoValidPaymentMethod, VinylNotAvailable
  Side-effects: Email sent to owner

    2. Domain Primitives I model the data shapes using simple AND/OR notation to capture constraints without the noise of classes or ORMs.

    data UnvalidatedOffer =
  UnvalidatedOfferMethod AND list of UnvalidatedVinyl

data CreditsForTrade = decimal number between 1 and 1000

    3. Feature Workflow I write a technology-free algorithm. This forces me to think about the process rather than the implementation.

    Input: UnvalidatedOffer
Step 1: ValidateVinyl
Step 2: ValidateCredits
Step 3: CreateOffer (atomic transaction)
Step 4: SendNotification

    4. Build: Double-Loop TDD

    I use Acceptance Test-Driven Development (ATDD) to drive the implementation from the outside in.

    The Outer Loop (Acceptance Tests) Before implementing, I write E2E tests based on the "Understand" phase. These tests are the contract between the customer and the code.

    • They must read as plain English behavior statements (BDD/Gherkin style).
    • Every failure state identified in Phase 3 becomes a test case here.

    The Inner Loop (Unit Tests) I write unit tests for the specific domain objects and services needed to make the outer loop pass.

    • Refactor: I clean up the code while keeping the tests green.
    • Done when: All tests are green, integration tests pass in CI, and the code is reviewed and verified in staging.

    My Core Principles

    • Features are the key: I organize code by feature/subdomain, not by technical layer (controllers/services). A feature is the only thing that matters; everything else is infrastructure.
    • Discover edge cases early: I find edge cases during the "Understand" phase, not in production.
    • Separation of concerns: If a test could fail due to something in a different layer, it is testing the wrong thing. I guard against testing implementation details in acceptance tests.
    • Ownership: I treat code as craft. I am responsible for every line of code shipped, ensuring it solves the essential problem without introducing accidental complexity.
